title: Should You Be Able to Bet on Reality TV?

blocks.content: We are still in the Wild West period of online gambling. Predictions market apps like Kalshi and Polymarket allow one to invest in the outcomes of events. Is that gambling? Colloquially, for sure. Legally, things are still a little squishy. The Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CTFC) took steps to firm up those squishy spots June 10, publishing a Notice of Proposed Rulemaking and seeking comment from the public. Under federal law, the CTFC can block transactions concerning terrorism, assassination, war, and “gaming” if they go against the public interest. The new rules seek to define “gaming,” and the CTCF is specifically asking the public if they think reality TV should fall under that definition.

Folks within the reality industry do want to see some sort of regulation of betting on their product. Jeff Probst called out Kalshi and Polymarket for “spoiling” the winner of Survivor 50. Probst told Variety these markets are “incentivizing people to lie, cheat and steal to get ahead,” basically blaming the markets for encouraging insider trading on reality TV. This also came up in February, when Kalshi itself accused an editor on MrBeast’s team of insider trading, for placing bets on what the YouTuber would or would not say in each video.

As the law currently stands, the CTFC can review or block “gaming” bets if they aren’t in the public interest. For example, you can bet on a football game, but you can’t bet on a football player getting injured as this would incentivize hurting the player. If reality competition shows fell under “gaming,” the CTFC could block bets on whether islanders get medivac’d off Survivor.

The proposed definition of “gaming” is this: “any activity that: (i) one or more participants typically engage in for purposes of recreation or to entertain others; (ii) is governed by rules; and (iii) includes measurable occurrences or outcomes that depend on the participants’ luck, skill, or athletic ability during the activity.” Sporting events are gaming because it’s entertainment, and because the outcome is determined that day. Award shows like the Tonys aren’t gaming, because the voters determine the outcome based on past displays of luck and/or skill. Laurie Metcalf isn’t leaving it all on the paint that night, “gitchie gitchie” notwithstanding.

You can probably already see why reality TV would be hard to categorize by this rule. On RuPaul’s Drag Race, judges are evaluating a queen’s performance that night, but also their development as a performer over the season. Would you call the Lip Sync for Your Life “gaming,” but not who won that week’s Snatch Game and runway? Genuinely, the Trump Administration is asking.
